title: Chapter 18: The Weight of Another's Pain
paragraphs: Ash fell upon Agdash slowly and inevitably, covering the ancient marble slabs with a shroud of oblivion. Six hours had passed since the four outcasts had lowered themselves to their knees before the barred Great Gates.

It was not a siege in the conventional sense, but for the city's defenders, this mute, motionless scene was becoming unbearable torture. In the logic of a dying world, everything was perfectly clear: enemies attack, cowards flee, the strong kill the weak. But a goblin sitting submissively in the radioactive dust under the aim of dozens of crossbows broke the very axis of their universe. His humility was louder than the war horns of Zakhan; it itched beneath the skin of the elven guards, irritating old spiritual wounds.

Kirah did not move. The gargantuan demon had turned into a monolithic statue of black bone and pitch, sheltering Yashil from the cutting wind. but inside him raged a primal, chest-tearing conflict. His nature, forged in the legions of the Void, demanded he tear the guards to pieces at the slightest threat. Only the fragile, almost illusory bond with his small green-skinned friend kept his inner darkness on a chain.
Janavar was frankly bored. The werewolf lazily sharpened a claw against the blade of his axe, periodically yawning so wide that his terrifying fanged arsenal was visible.

"Do you know what the problem with these long-lived ones is?" the wolf grumbled, nodding toward the wall. "They think too much. A wolf doesn't think when he sees a closed door. He either smashes it with his head or marks the territory and leaves. And these ones just stand and look at us like we're an unsolvable equation. We're breaking their matrix. Auf."
Renée, with an elegant flick of her fingers, tossed a smoldering butt into the ash and immediately pulled a fresh cigarette from a hidden pocket of her corset.

"They aren't thinking, flea-bag. They're afraid," the fox corrected coldly, lighting up from a tiny magical spark that flared on the tip of her claw. "We are a mirror to them. They look at the demon and see their coming death. They look at the goblin and see their own helplessness. No one likes mirrors, especially when the face is disfigured. Someone's nerves are going to snap soon. I'd bet my last golden bracelet on it."
The fox, as always, proved right.

The heavy small leaf of the gate, inscribed with protective runes, swung open with a long, groaning sound. From there, marching in step, came a squad of five elves in heavy plate. They were led by a young lieutenant. His once beautiful face was distorted by a nervous tic, and his fair hair was smeared with soot. In his eyes burned the wild, feverish hatred of a being with nothing left to lose.

The lieutenant approached the sitting group closely. He didn't even glance at the werewolf or the fox. His gaze flickered toward Kirah but stopped on Yashil. The goblin slowly raised his head, meeting that burning gaze with his calm, dark eyes.
"Get up, vous vile creature," the elf hissed. His voice trembled with suppressed rage. "Get up and take up your weapon. Give me a reason."
Yashil did not stir.
"I have no weapon, elf," he replied quietly. "And I have no one to fight here."
The lieutenant gasped with rage. This ostentatious peacefulness seemed like a sophisticated, poisonous mockery to him. He struck Yashil across the face with the heavy shaft of his halberd.

The sound of wood hitting flesh echoed over the wasteland. The small goblin collapsed onto his side into the gray dust. From the gash in his cheek, thick, dark-green blood splashed, instantly soaking into the dry earth.

"I said, resist!" breaking into an hysterical scream, the elf raised the halberd for a second blow. "You monsters took my sister in the eastern marches! You are devouring our world! Show your true face, freak!"
The air suddenly became heavy, like lead.

Kirah made a sound that made the blood run cold—a low, rumbling groan of a tectonic fault. The crimson veins on the demon's body flared with blinding light. A dark mania of destruction burst from him in a stifling wave, melting the ash beneath his paws. The titan rose to his full height with a roar, shielding the wounded goblin. His claws elongated, turning into black sabers.
The guards on the wall raised their crossbows in panic. Janavar gripped his axe with a clang, his pupils narrowing into vertical slits.
"You touched the weak—you woke the wolf," the werewolf growled, his muscles tensing like steel cables. "Now I'll rip your throat out, long-ears. Auf."
Renée froze, her cigarette poised a millimeter from her lips. Death stood at their doorstep, ready to reap a bountiful harvest.
But before the demon or the wolf could make a deadly lunge, a small green hand, stained with ash and blood, rested on Kirah's scorched leg.
"No, Kirah. Stand down," Yashil's voice was quiet, but it carried such undeniable, ancient power that the thousand-ton demon froze as if struck by lightning.

The goblin slowly, breathing heavily, rose from the ground. He did not wipe the blood streaming down his chin. Yashil stepped forward, pushing aside the frozen demon, and once again stood face to face with the heavily breathing lieutenant. The elf gripped the halberd so tightly his knuckles were white, but he couldn't take his eyes off the green eyes that held not a drop of judgment.

"You don't strike me because I took your sister," Yashil said with an infinite, heart-wrenching softness. "You strike me because you cannot reach the Void. Because the heavens that tore your life apart have no face to spit in. But here I am. I am here, I am ugly, and I am easy to hate."
The elf's hands trembled slightly. The tip of his halberd began to slowly lower toward the ground.

"If my blood could return your sister," Yashil took another step forward, baring his unprotected chest to the very blade, "strike. If my death could patch the hole in the heavens above Agdash—kill me right now. I will not defend myself. But we both know... that would only make the emptiness inside you even wider."

The lieutenant looked at the goblin, and his world collapsed. All his rage, all his hatred, nurtured through years of hopeless war, shattered to pieces against the wall of this impossible, absurd compassion. The elf made a strangled, pitiful sound, something like a sob, dropped his halberd into the ash, and, covering his face with his hands, sank to his knees. A great warrior of an ancient race was weeping before a goblin, unable to bear the weight of his own grief.

The guards on the wall froze, paralyzed by what they saw. Janavar slowly lowered his axe, blinking in surprise. Renée, whose face for a moment lost the mask of eternal cynicism, quietly exhaled a stream of smoke.
"By the spirits of the ancestors..." the fox whispered. "This idiot really knows how to win without baring a blade."

At that moment, a deep, resonating sound of a huge silver gong rang out above Agdash. It came from the highest point of the city, from where the spires of the inner palace were hidden among the clouds of ash.
The leaves of the Great Gates shuddered and, with a monumental clank of gears, began to slowly part, opening the way into the fading stronghold.
